speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Ita Gordon-- I-T-A G-O-R-D-O-N. I'm conducting an interview with Marcel Baum. Today's October 19, 1995. We're in Westminster, California, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Action.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Marcel Baum-- B-A-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Germany, in Bad Kreuznach. I was born there because my mother was in the hospital because of an illness of my father. My original hometown is Laufersweiler-- L-A-U-F-E-R-S-W-E-I-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born March 16, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moritz Baum-- M-O-R-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Laura-- L-A-U-R-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a butcher and [? handlesman, ?] what they call in Germany tradesman-- tradesman, trading with cattle. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, what was her--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a brother and a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is Alex Baum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alice Hanau, now. Her marriage name is Hanau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I grew up in Laufersweiler as a-- when my mother came back from Kreuznach, I was a very ill child. My mother's milk did not nourish me enough, so I was fairly ill until the age of three months. My mother took a trip to her parents. My mother's parents lived in France, in Vic-Sur-Seille-- V-I-C S-U-R S-E-I-L-L-E. And when my mom-- grandma saw me, she felt sorry and she got good cow milk, and that made me come back at life. And I went back to Laufersweiler until-- until my-- my-- and then my father passed away in 19-- on August 29, 1925. So my mother was a widow at the age of 20-- 25 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a year and a half old. And, naturally, I never knew my father. My mother was left alone with two children, and a father-in-law, who lived in the same house. And it was very difficult for a young woman at that age to make a-- she had to maintain the-- the household and make a living for the family. So she made-- she was strong and sustained the-- She overcame the debt of my father, and she kept the business going and-- and she took care of the father-in-law and us two children. We had a maid, and we had a-- like a butcher in the house to maintain the-- the butcher shop, because we also had a butcher shop in the-- in the family. Our house was very large. My mother had butcher shop, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], like people who would stay in town and want to stay overnight. We had several rooms to stay there. And she had a-- a tavern--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --serving liquor and beer. So my mother worked very hard to keep us children going.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your-- your mother's father-in-law, your grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Leopold Baum. And I-- Leopold Baum, yes. My-- my mother met my father in Laufersweiler for one reason, because my-- the parents of my mother were French, and she, too, after the First World War. And actually Vic-Sur-Seille was German before the First World War and became France after the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was almost on the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was on the border--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a borderline, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Borderline, of Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother had to move to Laufersweiler. My grandparents had-- was a family of five people, of five youngsters. My mother was the oldest. And my-- my grandma had a sister living in Laufersweiler, and she was a neighbor of the Baum family, where my father lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother had-- had to maintain the household for the older people. And-- excuse me. And do the-- the housework and keeping busy and taking care of them. And naturally my mother had a friend, my sister-- my father's sister and her became very good friend. So my father spent a lot of time in-- in the Baum's house, and that's how they met. And then in 1922, they decided to get married. And first came-- they-- they married on March the 22nd, 1922. My brother was born on December 30, 1922. Then my father became ill and then I-- ill, and then he was well again, and then I was born then, in March of '24, but then he became so ill in-- in 1924 that my mother had to stay with him in the hospital for a while in Kreuznach. And that's why I was born in Kreuznach. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back to Laufersweiler, I grew up in a nice family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And at the age of four or five, my mother sent me to France for a vacation. And I stayed in France for about a year and I went to Catholic kindergarten school. And when I had to end the regular school, my mother took me back and sent me to German school. So I hardly spoke any German anymore when I got back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this German school in Laufersweiler?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Laufersweiler. And in Laufersweiler, there were two-- two schools that were separated between Catholic and Protestant. The Jewish kids all went to the Protestant school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Laufersweiler? What was the nearest bigger-- bigger-- bigger town near Laufersweiler?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in between [INAUDIBLE] and if you recall, [INAUDIBLE] not far from Trier, either, on the Moselle. But Laufersweiler is in the [INAUDIBLE]. So it's a different area. [INAUDIBLE] and the Moselle is two different areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two different areas. So you were saying that there were two types of schools--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Laufersweiler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laufersweiler, mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, tell me more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so we-- we-- all Jewish kids went to the Protestant school. But we also had in Laufersweiler a beautiful temple. And we-- the Jewish kids would have a teacher coming once or twice a week and teach us Hebrew, reading Hebrew, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in addition to the regular school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that? When-- when did you have those classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started out at the age of seven, eight years old. We started to get basic religious. But actually, the-- we had a beautiful temple in Laufersweiler. It was very conservative because the men were downstairs and the ladies were-- were upstairs. And-- but we had no rabbi. The old-- the elderly men would conduct always the services. They all were very well educated in-- in reading the Hebrew. They have told us the Torah and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I grew up in Laufersweiler, it was difficult to go to school. You know, in 1933, '34, the Hitler came to power, and the children hated all the Jewish boys. And for-- also the teachers, they hated the Jewish kids because the Jewish kids would raise their hand in school when-- to answer the questions of the teacher, and the teacher would say, why do-- does the Jewish kids always have to answer the questions and not you kids? He would put us down all the time. We weren't allowed to express ourself in school. But before that, before Hitler came into power--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was it different? And how was it different? Was going to school different?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only went to school in 1930, so there are only two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we didn't see any difference between the children being mingled, and we were all friends and-- and enjoyed ourselves. We played soccer, we-- and we played in the [GERMAN] that's a sport. And we enjoyed it. But once Hitler came, we were denied all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our family was religious, but not over religious. I mean, we would observe the Shabbos, the Sabbath Friday night, and the Shabbos in service. And it was sometimes even difficult for my mother to observe the Sabbos because of the business she was in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have grandparents nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents were in France, in Vic-Sur-Seille.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your mother's side.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my mother's side. And my grandfather from my father's side lived with us in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is one of the memories you have of a Jewish celebration at home? What did your mother cook, some of the foods that she prepared?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- at-- she used to cook the brisket and on Pesach, we used to make the matzo ball soup. We used to have chicken every Friday night. That was a-- a tradition. [LAUGHS] And-- and if you know a [? shallot? ?] You know what [? shallot ?] is? That's a potato kugel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: She used to cook that every Friday night, so that was a tradition.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the family, yeah. Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Laufersweiler, did it have a lot of Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just read up on Laufersweiler the other day. The first Jews moved into Laufersweiler in 1751. And in order to move into that town, they had to pay a tax in-- in those days, all the Jews. And when I grew up, we had about 25 Jewish families. We were about 100 Jewish people living  in Laufersweiler. Laufersweiler was a fairly large, large Jewish community because we were about the only big temple in its surrounding of 20, 30 miles. The other towns had very little shuls, just a little room to-- to go on Saturdays to make-- if they had enough people to make minyan. But most of the time, Laufersweiler always had a minyan. We-- they used to go to Friday night service, Saturday night, and Saturday afternoon. We-- we observed all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would the relationships between neighbors, non-Jewish neighbors and Jewish neighbors, all right? What were they like before?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, when-- when we lived in Laufersweiler, my mother was very well liked-- first because she lost her husband, and then the people would help her so that she could stay in business and support her family. Even the Gentiles, they would always shop by her and come to the-- to the [INAUDIBLE] have a glass of beer, or a schnapps. And we-- the-- the Gentile families on our surrounding were very, very nice to-- to our family, to our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you start experiencing anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Semitism started in 1936-- in 1934, I mean. And then that started, my mother sent my brother to France. And I could hardly go to school without being prepared with a bag like they have today, those bags for the books to put in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Backpacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had these already in Germany in 1933, '34. So when I went to school, I never put the bag on my shoulders, I always left it loose and put the wrapping from the shoulder wraps on my hand to be prepared to fight. I always was ready to fight. Because they would come down on the school, out-- out of the school, and they would call us [GERMAN] and all kinds of names and would pick on us and would sometimes hit us. And I was always ready to hit them back and nobody could get close to me because I always swung my bag to-- to make sure that I don't get hurt. I did get hurt badly. I had a broken nose because of being hit with a stick at the one time. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, after school, after school, yes. Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your next memory--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of the anti-Semitism in--?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I have to go back a little bit. After my father passed away in 1924-- no-- it's in-- in August of '25, in August of '25, my-- my father passed away. And actually, my mother supported us. But before my father passed away, there was a man in town who became our second father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] His-- [CRYING] His name was [? Isi ?] Joseph. And they used to have the [INAUDIBLE], so he became from a very religious background because they-- they had to keep always kosher and everything else. And also, my father and my second father, very close friends. So my mother always said, before my father passed away, that he actually asked him to marry her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1929, my mother got remarried. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my sister was born in first of July 1930. And we-- my-- we moved in from my original home-- house, where my father lived in the [INAUDIBLE], my father couldn't handle the [INAUDIBLE] because he was an invalid of the First World War. He was shot in the leg, and all of his bicep was shot off. So the only thing he had was skin and bones from here to down here. So in order for him to walk, he used to walk-- used to wear a brace with a special shoe. That was Moritz Baum? That was-- that was [? Isi-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Isi-- Isi Joseph. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your second father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my second father.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Isi ?] Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Isi ?] Joseph, yeah. So he-- he-- we moved in-- into the-- the-- the new house that was actually also a Baum's house, but was a part of my Leopold house. And they had like a grocery store and selling materials. You know, in Europe, everybody used to make their own clothes and things like this. So we moved into that house. And we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lived there very comfortable, too. And also, like-- like I said, we'd observed Friday night and Saturday night, and-- and Shabbos and all that. And actually we had to be even more religious because my father also was the son of the matzo baker--

speaker: interviewer
text: The matzo.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who used to make the matzos. So we had to be also religious. So we-- I would say I had a very good life in Europe. I mean, nothing was missing. I had-- I was the first boy in town to have a bicycle and all that. But it was very difficult to live, naturally, when Hitler came to power, after-- after my mother was remarried, and the business was very good in beginning, in-- from 1930 to 1933. And then business went down.

speaker: interviewer
text: The farmers were not allowed to come into shop by us anymore. We used to make deliveries and used to do things for the farmers, and they all-- my mother was a good-hearted woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And everybody loved her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Even some of the farmer would come at night and shop at us because of my mother. Because she also grew up with some of these people, you know? And she went to school with some of these people. So it-- it was very, very difficult, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, from 1932 to '33, everything went all right. But then when Hitler came to power, things-- things were very bad. We couldn't-- we couldn't do this, we couldn't do that. And it-- everything was destroyed, the-- the closeness with all the rest of the people in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memory of any particular skirmish or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, not really. So we lived in that town until 1938. My father left 1934 because my father was a very high-strung person, very emotional, very high-strong. So my mother sent my father to France to our grandparents in Vic-Sur-Seille.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your grandparents' names in Vic-Sur-Seille?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henri Lippman and Dorothy Lippman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was my mother's parents. And then my mother had three brothers-- Andre, Fernand, and George-- and a sister. And my uncles, Andre, George, and Fernand actually lived with my grandparents. They supported, actually, my grandparents, and made a living-- good living. And then my brother went there, and he lived with my grandparents. Naturally, he was a spoiled brat. [LAUGHS] And he-- he went to school in France, but I didn't have the chance. So we were-- I was in school in Germany until '38. After the-- After I finished school, eighth grade, my parents finally decided to send us because it was so bad in Germany-- children couldn't go out, we couldn't do this, we couldn't do that. So my parents finally sent both my sister and I to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that, did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a bar mitzvah when I was 13. And it was my father who taught me the whole bar mitzvah. He knew a  lot. He-- he-- he even taught my children their bar mitzvahs. [CRYING] So I have to say, I have good memory of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: The teacher that you mentioned also, that came to teach you Hebrew, was he from the town, or did he have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he came from--

speaker: interviewer
text: --come from a different town?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came from the town of [? Simon. ?] [? Simon ?] was a bigger town, and they had acquired a big Jewish population. So he came only twice a week. So he had to teach there, and he had to teach there. So, and you know, in those days, the people didn't have anymore not that much money, either, anymore, because of Hitler, they weren't allowed to do the business like they used to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did all of this take place in the synagogue, the teaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had two separate rooms where the teacher would teach us. I even have a picture of all the children who were [LAUGHS] in-- in Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father taught you and helped you with the bar mitzvah in the synagogue or at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, at home, at home. He did that at home. Yeah. But when it came to read the Torah, then naturally we would go to the synagogue and open up the Torah, and would learn-- read out of the Torah in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that in 1938, you and your sister then had to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leave to France, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what that day was like, what you remember of the day you had to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was the most joyable day because, you know, we knew now we're going to become free. We can go out again, and-- and-- and play with other schoolmates, or-- or enjoy ourselves again. We didn't have to be afraid to be hit or to be called names, to-- the dirty Jew, or anything like this. So that was joyable.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I left Germany so that you know before the [SPEAKING GERMAN], the Kristallnacht. Do you know what the Kristallnacht is? Could you tell us what Kristallnacht is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Kristallnacht is when the Germans came in in 1938, and they destroyed all the temples, took out the Sefer Torah in Laufersweiler, and all that, in the middle of the street and marched on it. And broke all the furnitures in our house, and in our grandparents' house from my father's side. And that was very, very, very difficult on everybody. So then everybody-- we tried to get out already, out of Germany, in 1937, '38. We asked for-- people from the United States. We couldn't go to France, but we asked for the people from the United States to make us paper. But then nothing has happened. The quota, you know, the quota was so slow, so we-- we couldn't immigrate to the United States. So that's why we decided to go to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Kristallnacht--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's a good time to stop.
